title: Bitcoin Core internals
summary: How the reference client actually works under the hood. The UTXO set, transaction validation, mempool policy, the consensus rules, block templates, the P2P network, initial block download. What a node does on every block.

edges.label: UTXO model versus account model
edges.summary: Why Bitcoin uses unspent transaction outputs instead of an account ledger. How transactions are structured. What the chainstate database actually contains. Implications for parallelism, privacy, and validation.

edges.label: The mempool, fee estimation, and RBF
edges.summary: What the mempool is, how policy filters differ from consensus rules, replace-by-fee semantics, child-pays-for-parent, why the mempool is per-node rather than global, fee estimation strategies.

edges.label: Consensus rules, soft forks, hard forks
edges.summary: What makes a block or transaction valid in the strict consensus sense, the difference between soft forks (tightening rules) and hard forks (loosening or changing rules), why soft forks are the dominant upgrade mechanism.

edges.label: Block templates, the coinbase, and witness commitments
edges.summary: How a miner assembles a candidate block from the mempool, the coinbase transaction's special status, the segwit witness commitment, BIP-9 / BIP-8 version-bits signaling.

edges.label: Peer discovery, addrman, and eclipse defenses
edges.summary: How nodes find each other (DNS seeds, addrman, fixed seeds), the gossip protocol for blocks and transactions, ban scoring, the eclipse-attack threat model and the defenses against it.

edges.label: IBD, chainstate, indexing options
edges.summary: Initial block download, the chainstate (UTXO) database, pruning, the txindex / coinstatsindex / blockfilterindex options, assumeutxo, what a fresh node actually does on first start.
